subject: Cramer's rule, inverse matrix, and volume
concepts: Formula for A⁻¹ via Cofactors: The inverse of a matrix A is given by A⁻¹ = (1/det(A)) · C^T, where C is the matrix of cofactors and C^T is its transpose (called the classical adjugate). This formula works exactly when det(A) ≠ 0, which is also the condition for A to be invertible. In plain terms, each entry of A⁻¹ is built from (n−1)×(n−1) determinants (cofactors) of A, all divided by the full determinant of A.

Cofactors and Why A · C^T = det(A) · I: To verify the inverse formula, one checks that A · C^T = det(A) · I. The diagonal entries work because each row of A dotted with its own cofactors reproduces the cofactor expansion for det(A). The off-diagonal entries are zero because dotting row i of A with the cofactors of a different row j is equivalent to computing the determinant of a matrix with two identical rows, which is always zero.

Cramer's Rule: The solution to Ax = b can be written component-by-component as x_j = det(B_j) / det(A), where B_j is the matrix A with its j-th column replaced by the right-hand side vector b. This gives an explicit algebraic formula for each unknown. While elegant for theoretical purposes, Cramer's Rule is computationally impractical because computing n+1 determinants is far slower than Gaussian elimination.

The Determinant as Volume of a Parallelepiped (Box): The absolute value of det(A) equals the volume of the parallelepiped (box) whose edges are the row vectors of A. In 2D, |det(A)| is the area of the parallelogram formed by the two row vectors; in 3D, |det(A)| is the volume of the box formed by the three row vectors. The sign of the determinant indicates orientation (right-handed vs. left-handed), but not volume.

Why Determinant = Volume: The proof works by showing that the volume function satisfies the same three defining properties as the determinant. Property 1: the unit cube (A = I) has volume 1 = det(I). Property 2: swapping two edges changes orientation but not volume magnitude. Property 3a: scaling one edge by t scales volume by t, matching the determinant's linearity in each row. Property 3b: the linearity-in-a-row property of the determinant matches the way area/volume splits when an edge is written as a sum of two vectors.

Determinant of an Orthogonal Matrix is ±1: If Q is an orthogonal matrix (Q^T Q = I), then taking determinants of both sides gives det(Q^T) · det(Q) = 1, and since det(Q^T) = det(Q), we get det(Q)² = 1, so det(Q) = ±1. Geometrically, orthogonal matrices represent rotations or reflections of the unit cube, which preserve volume but may flip orientation.

Area of a Triangle from Vertex Coordinates: The area of a triangle with one vertex at the origin and the other two at (a, b) and (c, d) is (1/2)|ad − bc|. For a general triangle with vertices (x₁,y₁), (x₂,y₂), (x₃,y₃), the area is (1/2)|det([[x₁,y₁,1],[x₂,y₂,1],[x₃,y₃,1]])|. These formulas use only vertex coordinates and avoid computing bases, heights, or angles.
examples: Verifying the 2×2 inverse formula: for A = [[a,b],[c,d]], the formula A⁻¹ = (1/det(A))[[d,−b],[−c,a]] is confirmed by identifying entries as cofactors — d is the (1,1) cofactor (strike row 1, col 1), −b is the cofactor of c (strike row 2, col 1, with a minus sign because 2+1 is odd), and so on.

Checking A · C^T on the diagonal for a 3×3 matrix A = [[a11,...,a13],...]: the (1,1) entry of A · C^T is a11·c11 + a12·c12 + a13·c13, which is exactly the cofactor expansion of det(A) along row 1, giving det(A). The same applies to each diagonal entry.

Checking A · C^T off the diagonal: the (1,2) entry uses row 1 of A with cofactors from row 2. This equals the determinant of the matrix [[a,b],[a,b]] (first and second rows both equal to row 1 of A), which has two identical rows, so its determinant is zero — confirming the off-diagonal entries of A · C^T are zero.

Cramer's Rule applied to a 3×3 system Ax = b: x₁ = det(B₁)/det(A), where B₁ is A with its first column replaced by b; x₂ = det(B₂)/det(A), where B₂ is A with its second column replaced by b; and so on.
Volume of the unit cube from A = I: the three row vectors are the standard basis vectors e₁, e₂, e₃; they form a unit cube of volume 1, and det(I) = 1, confirming the formula.
Volume of the box from an orthogonal matrix Q: Q's rows are orthonormal, so the parallelepiped is a rotated unit cube, still with volume 1; det(Q) = ±1, and |det(Q)| = 1 confirms the formula.

Doubling the first row of a matrix doubles the volume of the box (property 3a): if the first edge of the box is doubled, the box volume doubles; correspondingly, factoring 2 out of row 1 of the determinant also doubles it, showing consistency.
Area of a parallelogram with edges (a, b) and (c, d) in 2D: area = |ad − bc| = |det([[a,b],[c,d]])|, avoiding any computation of lengths, heights, or angles.
Area of a triangle with vertices at the origin, (a, b), and (c, d): area = (1/2)|ad − bc|.

Area of a general triangle with vertices (x₁,y₁), (x₂,y₂), (x₃,y₃): area = (1/2)|det([[x₁,y₁,1],[x₂,y₂,1],[x₃,y₃,1]])|; subtracting rows kills the third column's ones and reduces the problem to the origin-based formula.

Why Cramer's Rule is impractical: for a 1024×1024 system, one would need to compute 1025 determinants, each requiring enormous computation, whereas Gaussian elimination solves the system directly and efficiently.
analogy: Cramer's Rule is compared to a book titled 'Mathematics for the Million' that praised it as the go-to method for solving linear systems — the lecturer reframes this as 'Mathematics for the Millionaire' because only someone with unlimited resources (time and money) could afford the astronomically expensive computation it requires, whereas Gaussian elimination solves the same problem almost instantly.

The parallelepiped (box) formed by the rows of a matrix is compared to a familiar physical box: the identity matrix gives the standard unit cube, and an orthogonal matrix Q gives the same unit cube simply rotated in space — same shape, same volume, just pointing in a different direction.

The formula A⁻¹ = (1/det(A)) C^T is contrasted with the Gauss-Jordan elimination algorithm: elimination is described as an efficient numerical procedure that finds A⁻¹ without revealing any structure, while the cofactor formula is the algebraic 'x-ray' that shows exactly what each entry of A⁻¹ is made of.
reviewed_transcript: What if you could solve any linear system just by computing determinants? That's exactly what Cramer's Rule promises — and it's gorgeous. Take the system with matrix A equals [2,1; 3,4] and right-hand side [5; 11]. Want the first unknown? Just swap column one with your target vector, take that new determinant, and divide by det(A). You get nine-fifths — no row reduction needed. The same elegant idea powers the inverse matrix formula: A-inverse equals one over det(A), times the transpose of the cofactor matrix — also called the adjugate. For a 2×2, that's why the inverse flips the diagonal, negates the off-diagonal, and scales by one over ad-minus-bc. Think of cofactors as the matrix's own DNA — each one encodes how the whole structure changes when you eliminate one row and one column and take the resulting determinant. Here's the catch: computing determinants this way costs factorial time, so for large systems, elimination wins every time. But master these ideas, and you hold the theoretical keys to understanding why inverses exist, when they break down, and how geometry — area, volume — lives inside every determinant.
